claim: The duplex steel was hot rolled to 6 mm, solution treated at 1050 °C for 0.5 h and water quenched, cold rolled to 3 mm thickness, then annealed at 600–1050 °C for 1–5 min and water quenched; the resulting specimens are designated DPXXX–Y (XXX = annealing temperature, Y = annealing time in min).
evidence_text: The HR plates were heated to 1050 ◦C and isothermally held for 0.5 h, quenched in water and then cold rolled (CR) to 3 mm thickness. Next, the CR sheets were annealed at the temperatures ranged from 600 ◦C to 1050 ◦C for the various periods in the range of 1–5 min and then quenched in water again. The resultant specimens are termed DPXXX–Y for easy discussion, where XXX and Y represent the annealing temperature and period respectively.

claim: In the DP950-5 specimen (annealed at 950 °C for 5 min), Cr₇C₃ carbides were identified by TEM.
evidence_text: Cr₇C₃ carbides were identified in DP950–5

claim: In DP1000-5 (annealed at 1000 °C for 5 min), intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles were identified with an average radius of 0.86 ± 0.20 nm and a volume fraction of 1.56%.
evidence_text: intragranular κ-carbide nanoparticles were identified in DP1000–5 (c). ... r: 0.86 ± 0.20 nm, f: 1.56%

claim: Two types of Cr7C3 carbide particles were observed: coarse particles of several micrometers located in ferrite bands, and particles of hundreds of nanometers precipitated at grain boundaries.
evidence_text: We observed two types of $\mathrm { { C r } _ { 7 } \mathrm { { C } _ { 3 } } }$ carbide particles: one has the coarse size of several micrometers and is located in ferrite bands (see Fig. S2a), the other has the size of hundreds of nanometers and precipitates at grain boundaries, see Fig. 1b.

claim: DP1000-5 achieved an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 1250 ± 15 MPa, total elongation (TE) of 41.4 ± 0.5%, and specific UTS (SUTS) of 196.5 ± 2.4 MPa·g⁻¹·cm³, with a recrystallized austenite fraction of 95.2%, γ grain size of 7.40 ± 5.13 μm, ferrite fraction by XRD of 17.8%, and EBSD α and δ fractions of 4.1% and 9.3%, respectively.

The transverse-direction specimen DP1000-5-TD exhibited significantly lower total elongation (18.1 ± 0.4%) compared to the rolling-direction DP1000-5 (41.4 ± 0.5%), with UTS of 1190 ± 15 MPa versus 1250 ± 15 MPa, indicating pronounced mechanical anisotropy.

DP950-5 showed high strength (UTS 1350 ± 15 MPa) but very low ductility (TE 5.5 ± 0.2%), coupled with a high ferrite fraction of ~36% as measured by EBSD (α 23.8%, δ 12.1%) and only 54.8% recrystallized austenite, indicating that the coarse ferrite and brittle intergranular carbides led to early fracture.
The recrystallized austenite grain size increases with annealing temperature and time, from 5.13 ± 4.74 μm in DP1000–1 to 7.40 ± 5.13 μm in DP1000–5 and 9.65 ± 8.17 μm in DP1050–1.
evidence_text: The Rexed austenite grain size increases from 5.13 ± 4.74 μm in DP1000–1 to 7.40 ± 5.13 μm in DP1000–5 and 9.65 ± 8.17 μm in DP1050–1, because the number and fraction of granular α ferrite both decrease with the increases in the annealing temperature and period, and the presence of the granular α ferrite grains at the boundaries and triple points can effectively retard the austenite grain growth.

claim: Annealing at 950 °C produces the coarsest δ and α grains, which partially transform back to austenite upon further temperature increase.
evidence_text: In summary, the annealing at 950 °C produces the coarsest δ and α grains (Fig. 2c1), which then partially transform back to austenite when the annealing temperature is further raised.

claim: Nanoindentation on DP1000–5 shows that ferrite is clearly softer than recrystallized austenite, which explains the lower yield strength in specimens annealed at 950 °C compared to those annealed at higher temperatures.
evidence_text: The nanoindentation results (see Fig. S4) on DP1000–5 show that ferrite is clearly softer than the Rexed austenite, leading to lower YS measured in the specimens annealed at 950 °C than those annealed at higher temperatures.

claim: When annealing temperature is raised from 950 °C to 1000 °C, the cold-rolled austenite becomes almost fully recrystallized and the total ferrite fraction decreases significantly, reducing the strength difference between FCC and BCC phases and the quantity of FCC/BCC interfaces, thereby improving both YS and TE through more compatible deformation.
evidence_text: When the annealing temperature increases from 950 ◦C to 1000 ◦C, the CR austenite is almost fully Rexed and the total ferrite fraction decreases significantly so that the difference in the strength of FCC and BCC and the quantity of FCC/BCC interfaces both decrease; thus, the harder austenite as the major phase contributes more to YS and both FCC/BCC phases can deform more compatibly when compared to the specimens annealed at 950 ◦C so that both YS and TE are greatly improved.

claim: Increasing annealing temperature to 1050 °C improves TE but remarkably impairs YS and UTS due to austenite grain growth and significant dissolution of Cr7C3 carbides and intergranular κ-carbides.
evidence_text: the further increase of the annealing temperature to 1050 ◦C causes TE improved but YS/UTS remarkably impaired, which are primarily because of the austenite grains growth (see Table 1) and the significant dissolution of Cr7C3 carbides/the intergranular κ-carbides, as revealed in Fig. 1a.

claim: DP1000–5 and DP1050–1 exhibit products of specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) and total elongation (TE) exceeding 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%, which is superior to 2000 series aluminum alloys (<4), Ti6Al4V (<4), duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (2.7–6.5), and austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (2.9–7.4).
evidence_text: Both DP1000–5 and DP1050–1 specimens exhibit clearly superior mechanical combination to the existing high-strength metallic materials, see Fig. 3b, because their products of SUTS and TE are more than 8 GPa·g^{-1}·cm^3·%; whilst less than 4 GPa·g^{-1}·cm^3·% for 2000 series aerospace aluminum alloys [34] and titanium alloy of Ti6Al4V [35], and in the ranges of 2.7-6.5 and 2.9-7.4 GPa·g^{-1}·cm^3·% for the duplex [12,13,16,19,20] and austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels [15,36], respectively.

claim: In DP1000–5, a tensile crack nucleates at an α/γ interface and propagates along ND until it encounters a δ ferrite band parallel to RD, where it is deflected to propagate along RD; the RD cracks may blunt within the soft δ ferrite bands through dislocation emission and local plastic dissipation, delocalizing plastic strain.
evidence_text: In Fig. 4a, a tensile crack in DP1000–5 was supposed to be nucleated at the α/γ interface and then propagate along the normal direction (ND) until it encountered a δ ferrite band parallel to the RD, where it was deflected to propagate along the RD. These RD cracks may propagate into the soft δ ferrite bands and then get blunt through dislocation emission and local plastic dissipation in vicinity of the crack tip [37], which significantly delocalized the plastic strain [38].

claim: In DP1000–5-TD, δ/γ interfaces are perpendicular to the tensile direction and subjected to high de-cohesive stress, causing crack initiation at both δ/γ and α/γ interfaces; these cracks quickly connect across the full thickness, leading to poor ductility.
evidence_text: In DP1000–5-TD, however, the δ/γ interfaces are perpendicular to the tensile direction (see Fig. 4b) and subjected to the high de-cohesive stress; consequently, cracks were initiated at these δ/γ interfaces in addition to the α/γ interfaces, and then propagated until they quickly got connected across the full thickness during straining, leading to poor ductility.

claim: The developed Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr duplex steel achieves an ultralow density of 6.36 g·cm⁻³ and a product of SUTS and TE exceeding 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·% through a duplex microstructure of granular α ferrite, δ ferrite bands, and a fully recrystallized austenitic matrix obtained by annealing cold-rolled structure at 1000 °C for 5 min.
evidence_text: we successfully developed a new Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr austenite-ferrite duplex steel having both the ultralow density of 6.36 g·cm^{-3} and the ultrahigh product of SUTS and TE exceeding 8 GPa·g^{-1}·cm^3·%. Such an unprecedented mechanical combination was achieved by the duplex microstructures comprising two types of ferrites, i.e., the granular α ferrite grains plus δ ferrite bands, and fully Rexed austenitic matrix, which was realized by the annealing of the CR structure at 1000 °C for 5 min.

claim: Granular α ferrite restrains austenite grain growth when present in sufficient amount, but cracks easily initiate at α/γ interfaces during deformation; these cracks can be captured and deflected by δ ferrite bands and even blunted within them.
evidence_text: The granular α ferrite grains can restrain the austenite grain growth when they are sufficient though cracks are also easily initiated at the α/γ interfaces during deformation; however, these cracks can be captured and deflected by δ ferrite bands, where they even get blunted.

claim: During annealing at 1000 °C, Cr7C3 carbides, intergranular κ-carbides, and ferrite significantly transform to austenite, enriching it in Mn, Al, and C, which promotes high-density nanosized κ-carbide precipitation upon water quenching.
evidence_text: Meanwhile, Cr7C3 carbides, the intergranular κ-carbides, and ferrite significantly transformed to austenite during the annealing at 1000 °C, resulting in high Mn/Al/C concentrations in austenite for the high-density nanosized κ-carbide precipitation during water quenching.

claim: The developed duplex steel achieves an ultralow density of 6.36 g·cm⁻³ and an ultrahigh product of specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) and total elongation (TE) exceeding 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%.
evidence_text: we successfully developed a new Fe-Mn-Al-C-Cr austenite-ferrite duplex steel having both the ultralow density of 6.36 g·cm⁻³ and the ultrahigh product of SUTS and TE exceeding 8 GPa·g⁻¹·cm³·%.

claim: The outstanding mechanical property combination was realized by annealing the cold-rolled (CR) structure at 1000 °C for 5 min.
evidence_text: which was realized by the annealing of the CR structure at 1000 °C for 5 min.

claim: The steel exhibits a duplex microstructure comprising granular α ferrite grains, δ ferrite bands, and a fully recrystallized (Rexed) austenitic matrix.
evidence_text: by the duplex microstructures comprising two types of ferrites, i.e., the granular α ferrite grains plus δ ferrite bands, and fully Rexed austenitic matrix

claim: Granular α ferrite grains restrain austenite grain growth but also provide α/γ interfaces where cracks easily initiate during deformation; however, these cracks can be captured, deflected, and blunted by δ ferrite bands.
evidence_text: The granular α ferrite grains can restrain the austenite grain growth when they are sufficient though cracks are also easily initiated at the α/γ interfaces during deformation; however, these cracks can be captured and deflected by δ ferrite bands, where they even get blunted.

claim: During annealing at 1000 °C, Cr₇C₃ carbides, intergranular κ-carbides, and ferrite significantly transform to austenite, resulting in high Mn/Al/C concentrations in austenite that promote high-density nanosized κ-carbide precipitation during water quenching.
evidence_text: Cr₇C₃ carbides, the intergranular κ-carbides, and ferrite significantly transformed to austenite during the annealing at 1000 °C, resulting in high Mn/Al/C concentrations in austenite for the high-density nanosized κ-carbide precipitation during water quenching.
